nativeRange.habitat: Forest-shaded, well-oxygenated headwaters with clear water; mixed sand and rock substrate with submerged driftwood and leaf litter. Documented in the Ataran River basin (Kayin State, Myanmar) and the Hanthayaw/Suriya River (Tak Province, Thailand) — both draining into the Salween/Ataran system.

diet.notes: Readily accepts quality sinking pellets, frozen or live bloodworm, Tubifex, and Artemia. Blanched vegetables (cucumber, courgette, spinach, melon) are generally accepted. Has been documented consuming aquatic snails, making it useful for pest-snail control. Earthworm can be offered sparingly. Wild diet is predominantly invertebrate-based but plant matter is consumed opportunistically.

avoidWith: small fish that may be harassed or eaten
slow-moving or long-finned species (bettas, fancy guppies)

summary: Striking Botiid loach from Myanmar and Thailand, known for its bold polka-dot or constellation pattern. Social, semi-aggressive, and prone to jumping — best kept in groups of 5+ in a covered, well-decorated tank.
careNotes:
Botia kubotai was formally described in 2004 by Kottelat, making it one of the newer Botiid species in the hobby. It is commonly sold as the Angelicus Loach or Polka Dot Loach, and occasionally as the Marble Loach or Cloud Botia.

Like all Botiids, this species is highly social and generally advised to be kept in groups of at least 5, with 10 or more often preferred for stable social hierarchies. Solitary specimens or very small groups have been known to become stressed, withdrawn, or show increased aggression toward tankmates. Typical intraspecific behavior includes dominance displays ('greying out' of body color), shadowing, and audible clicking sounds produced by the pharyngeal teeth and subocular spine grinding — this is normal behavior shared across the genus.

Pattern darkens significantly with maturity: juveniles display horizontal bars and stripes that gradually widen and develop into the distinctive polka-dot/constellation pattern as the fish grows. This ontogenetic color change means the fish sold as small juveniles can look quite different to adults.

Identification note: juveniles are sometimes confused with Botia histrionica (Burmese Loach), which has a similar pattern at small sizes. The brief also notes confusion with B. helodes (Reticulated Loach) in some trade contexts. Bar splitting and horizontal 'peaks' in the striped pattern are a distinguishing feature of B. kubotai as it matures.

This species has been known to jump, and a tight-fitting lid is generally considered essential. Sharp, motile sub-ocular spines can tangle in fine nets and pierce skin — a cup or container is recommended for transfers.

Like other scaleless or small-scaled Botiids, this species is generally considered sensitive to copper-based medications. Copper-free treatments should be used for disease management. They are also susceptible to 'Skinny Disease' caused by the flagellate parasite Spironucleus; Levamisole or Fenbendazole have been documented as treatments. Ich susceptibility is also noted by some keepers, especially at lower temperatures.

SeriouslyFish recommends a minimum tank base of 120 × 45 cm (approximately 243 L) for a group; the brief guidance of 100–150 L is more commonly cited for a starter group of 5. Given the active, semi-aggressive nature and adult size of up to 15 cm, a 150 L minimum with 120 cm length is a reasonable starting point, with larger tanks preferable for long-term keeping.

Weekly water changes of 30–50% are generally advised to maintain the water quality this species tends to need. Well-oxygenated water with moderate to strong filtration is preferred, reflecting the fast-moving headwater habitats of its native range.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is exceptionally rare. Only a single tank-bred specimen has been documented (2008), and commercial production is understood to rely on hormonal induction. Spontaneous spawning in home aquaria has not been reliably replicated. Conditioning in clean, slightly acidic water with varied live/frozen foods may be relevant, consistent with approaches used for related Botiids, but no established private-hobbyist breeding protocol exists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Aquarium Co-Op (slice primary) returned 404 for Botia kubotai; SeriouslyFish used as primary. Provides native range, water parameters (22–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, 36–179 ppm hardness), adult size 140–150 mm, minimum tank base 120×45 cm (~243 L), grouping, diet, temperament, and behavioral notes including escape risk and subocular spines.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Botiidae, order Cypriniformes, adult size 15 cm max (TL), native range Ataran River basin (Myanmar). IUCN Data Deficient. Used for taxonomy and size cross-check.

additional.name: Loaches.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (5 inches / ~13 cm), temperature (75–82°F = 24–28°C), pH 6.8–7.5, hardness soft-to-medium (max dH 20). Size slightly lower than SeriouslyFish (13 vs 14–15 cm); used broader range min:10–max:15 across sources. Temperature upper limit consistent.

fish.breedingNotes: Only one confirmed captive-bred specimen on record (2008). Commercial production relies on hormonal induction. No established protocol for spontaneous home-aquarium breeding.
